agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to review building permit issued under old R1 SSMUH rules
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to consider a building permit that was issued for 5305 Eglinton Street under the original R1 SSMUH Zoning District rules. The permit was approved because the application was submitted before the October 21, 2025 deadline, and the plans met the zoning rules in place at that time. Staff will continue to monitor the project for compliance, but the permit has already been issued. This is not a new decision — it is a review of a permit that was granted under the old rules.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action needed from you — this is a review of an already-issued permit, not a new proposal that affects your business.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.1
Glenn Jeffrey - 5305 Eglinton Street - Excessive Height
1.
Glenn Jeffrey - 5305 Eglinton Street Excessive Height.pdf
Copy: Chief Administrative Officer, General Manager Corporate Services, General Manager Planning and Development
Note from General Manager Planning and Development:
While staff will continue to monitor the noted Building Permit case for compliance, the application was made on or before the October 21, 2025, deadline for submissions under the original R1 SSMUH Zoning District. The plans submitted conform to the zoning bylaw at that time and a Building Permit has been issued on this basis.

attachments.title: Glenn Jeffrey - 5305 Eglinton Street Excessive Height.pdf

agenda_briefs.article_title: Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions Agenda — May 12, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby development cost charges bylaw update
agenda_briefs.summary: The Independent Contractors and Businesses Association (ICBA) has formally requested that Burnaby City Council adopt a temporary Development Cost Charge (DCC) reduction of 25 to 50 percent for a minimum of 24 months, citing precedents set by Surrey, Kelowna, and Metro Vancouver. The letter notes that combined DCCs on a Burnaby apartment unit now exceed $100,000 per home, and that a 23% contractor layoff rate and projected drop in BC housing starts to 30,000 in 2026 justify the reduction to restart the stalled condo pipeline.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you are a developer or property owner in Burnaby, review the ICBA proposal and consider contacting Council to express support or concerns before a decision is made.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.2
Independent Contractors and Businesses Association - DCC in Burnaby
1.
Independent Contractors and Businesses Association - Development Cost Charges in Burnaby.pdf
Copy
:
Chief Administrative Officer, General Manager Corporate Services, General Manager Planning and Development
Note from General Manager Planning and Development
: The Development Cost Charges (DCC) Bylaw, Bylaw No. 14645, and Amenity Cost Charges (ACC) Bylaw, Bylaw No. 14646, were adopted on June 24, 2024. DCCs and ACCs are collected to help fund growth‑related infrastructure and community amenities associated with new development. Rates are established based on long‑term growth forecasts and eligible capital programs and are periodically reviewed to reflect changes in development conditions and legislative requirements.

agenda_briefs.article_title: Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions Agenda — May 12, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Hospital HVAC noise may exceed city bylaw limits
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is considering a report on excessive noise from new HVAC units at Burnaby General Hospital. City staff found noise exceeds the 55 dBA limit at some locations, contradicting the contractor's earlier report. A resident reports constant loud noise affecting sleep and outdoor use, with audio/video recordings and decibel readings available. The hospital is provincially run but cooperating with the city.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you live or work near Burnaby Hospital and are affected by the noise, contact City Enforcement to report your concerns before the council decision.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.3
JR Grewal - Excessive HVAC Noise from Burnaby Hospital
1.
JR Grewal - Excessive HVAC noise from Burnaby Hospital.pdf
Copy: Chief Administrative Officer, General Manager Corporate Services, General Manager Community Safety
Note from General Manager Community Safety:
In the summer of 2025, the City began receiving noise complaints related to newly installed HVAC units at Burnaby General Hospital (BGH). As of February 26, a total of 19 complaints has been received regarding this issue. Following the initial complaint in 2025, City Enforcement staff sent a letter to Fraser Health requesting they investigate the source of the noise. On March 3, 2026, the contractor responsible for installing the units, EllisDon, provided an acoustic report to staff advising the noise measured between 50 dBA and 54 dBA, which is below the bylaw requirement of 55 dBA. In their response EllisDon also committed to investigating the possibility of adding acoustic treatment in the area to further reduce noise levels.
After receiving the acoustic report staff contacted each complainant and advised that the reported noise level did comply with the Burnaby Noise Bylaw. Staff also informed the complainants that additional options to further mitigate noise from the HVAC equipment are being considered; however, this process may take some time. Noise measurements taken by staff as recently May 1, 2026, were inconsistent with the acoustic report provided by EllisDon. At some locations the continuous noise levels complied; however, at another location the noise exceeded the 55 dBA permitted under the bylaw. Staff will follow up with the complainant and report the findings to Fraser Health.
It should be noted that Provincial Governments do not typically have to comply with municipal bylaws. BGH is cooperating with the City in order to find a solution.

attachments.title: JR Grewal - Excessive HVAC noise from Burnaby Hospital.pdf

agenda_briefs.article_title: Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions Agenda — May 12, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to consider automated cart recycling collection
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposal to modernize curbside recycling by switching to automated cart collection. The Engineering Department has reviewed this option for years and notes potential benefits, but also warns of higher contamination rates (when non-recyclable items are mixed in) and possible penalties from Recycle BC, the organization that manages recycling programs in BC. Council will consider whether to move forward with this change, which could affect how businesses and residents handle their recycling.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could change how your business manages recycling waste.
agenda_briefs.industries: Retail, Food Service, Grocery & Food Retail
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.4
Susan Tam - Modernizing Curbside Recycling: Moving to Automated Carts in Burnaby
1.
Susan Tam - Modernizing Curbside Recycling Moving to Automated Carts in Burnaby.pdf
Copy: Chief Administrative Officer, General Manager Corporate Services, General Manager Engineering
Note from General Manager Engineering
: The Engineering Department has been reviewing the option of automated cart collection for many years. While there are benefits as the resident describes, there are also some other limitations to consider such as higher material contamination rates, risk of penalty from Recycle BC, and others. The department will continue to evaluate collection options which provide the best overall combination of efficiencies and service levels.

attachments.title: Susan Tam - Modernizing Curbside Recycling Moving to Automated Carts in Burnaby.

agenda_briefs.article_title: Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions Agenda — May 12, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to review policy for daycare applications
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is considering a request from Axin Architecture Ltd. to change the daycare application process. Currently, Burnaby requires Fraser Health approval before accepting a building permit application; the request asks that applications be accepted and reviewed without that approval upfront, with permit issuance conditional on later receipt of Fraser Health approval. The request also asks the city to move to fully digital submissions, as in-person hard-copy submissions are still required. The matter has been referred to the Planning Committee for further review.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the Planning Committee meeting date — this could affect future daycare applications in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

agenda_briefs.article_title: Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions Agenda — May 12, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Student housing rezoning at Rathburn Drive, Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on REZ #25-19 to rezone land on Rathburn Drive for a purpose-built student housing development. The applicant reports over 40 letters of support and an online petition with 380+ signatures. The developer was instructed on December 10, 2025 to refine the proposal addressing environmental risks (wildfire, flooding, Stoney Creek proximity), unit sizes, rental vs. student unit mix, and occupant verification. The project is listed on Burnaby's Major Projects Listing for the Lougheed area.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Monitor the council meeting schedule and attend any public hearing to voice support or concerns about this student housing rezoning.

key_dates.label: Council instruction to refine proposal — Burnaby REZ #25-19

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed student housing development on Rathburn Drive heads to Planning Committee
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to refer a proposed student housing development on Rathburn Drive to the Planning Committee for further review. The application, submitted by Veda and Larry Yee, proposes approximately 1,400 student housing units in a low-density, family-oriented neighbourhood near Stoney Creek. The Planning Committee will examine the proposal and make a recommendation to Council before any final decision is made.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the Planning Committee meeting date and consider attending to have your say on this proposed development.

agenda_briefs.article_title: Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions Agenda — May 12, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to consider Minister's response on SSMUH in Lower Westridge
agenda_briefs.summary: The Minister of Housing and Municipal Affairs has responded to Burnaby's March 3, 2026 letter about SSMUH in Lower Westridge. The Minister confirms that local governments may exempt lands from SSMUH density only where a qualified professional demonstrates that developing to the required density would significantly increase hazard risk with no practical mitigation. In the absence of such a professional assessment, pre-emptively restricting new home construction across broader areas is not permitted. The Minister expects Burnaby to rely on professional assessments rather than broad restrictions.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for council's decision on whether to commission a professional hazard assessment for Lower Westridge — this will determine if SSMUH exemptions apply.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby parking fee increase opposed by resident
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved a city-wide blanket rate increase for on-street parking to $4 per hour and $10 per day, effective early 2026. The change will directly impact residents who park near SkyTrain stations to commute downtown for work or school, effectively doubling commuting costs for many.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect your parking costs if you operate a business with customer or employee parking in Burnaby.

key_dates.label: Burnaby on-street parking rate increase takes effect

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Four-plex zoning changes could affect Eglinton Street neighbourhood
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to consider proposed zoning changes that would allow four-plex housing on Eglinton Street. The proposal, brought forward by Doug and Audrey Robinson, would change the rules for what kind of homes can be built in that neighbourhood. If approved, the new bylaw would let property owners build up to four units on a single lot, instead of just one or two. This is still a proposal — Council has not made a final decision yet. The City's Chief Administrative Officer and planning staff are reviewing the idea before it goes to a vote.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to tell Council how the change would affect your property or business.

agenda_briefs.article_title: Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions Agenda — May 12, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby policy shift on inclusionary housing rules
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is considering a policy change that eliminates the one-to-one replacement requirement for demolished rental housing and replaces it with a reduced inclusionary rental delivery requirement. Under the new framework, inclusionary rents are capped at CMHC market median levels (not 20% below), requiring one-bedroom tenants to have incomes of $64,520–$96,780 — up from the previous $58,000 HIL threshold. This would exclude most households in the lower half of Burnaby's renter income distribution from the inclusionary program.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and attend the public hearing to voice concerns about the elimination of rental replacement requirements and reduced affordability thresholds.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby water restrictions — council to vote on new rules
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would change water restrictions in Burnaby. The proposal, submitted by Tessy Chalissery, would set new limits on when and how businesses and residents can use water outdoors, such as for watering lawns or washing cars. If approved, the new rules could affect how you manage your property or business during dry months. The decision has not been made yet — council will discuss and vote on it at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date — attend or submit comments to have your say on the proposed water restriction changes.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Construction & Development, Agriculture

agenda_briefs.article_title: Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions Agenda — May 12, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to consider SSMUH policy revision
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is considering a revision to the Small-Scale Multi-Unit Housing (SSMUH) policy based on public comments. The revision proposes: waiving development fees for small lots (4,000 sq ft or less); allowing one garage without counting it toward building area; increasing the impervious surface limit on small lots from 60% to 70%; and allowing a three-story building on a 4,000 sq ft lot without requiring more than three units. A final decision has not been made.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if approved, these changes could reduce costs and increase project feasibility for small-lot developments in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Liquor licence amendment for Steamworks Brewpub at 3845 William Street
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council will vote on a proposed liquor licence amendment (LLA #26-02) for Steamworks (Burnaby) at 3845 William Street. The amendment would allow liquor service on an outdoor patio under their Manufacturer – Brewery Licence with Lounge Area Endorsement. Public input is invited until May 11, 2026.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Submit written comments to planning@burnaby.ca or via Burnaby.ca/SubmitALetter by 9:00 am on May 11, 2026, referencing LLA#26-02.

key_dates.label: Public comment deadline — Steamworks LLA #26-02 patio licence

agenda_briefs.article_title: Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions Agenda — May 12, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning for development at 3900 Grand Promenade, Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on April 28, 2026 at 5 pm on proposed rezoning (REZ #24-23) for 3900 Grand Promenade, 9850 Austin Road, and 9858/9898 Gatineau Place. The amendment would replace some commercial office space with hotel and restaurant uses, add 362 market strata units, and increase Tower 1B by 7 storeys and Tower 1A by 4 storeys. No public hearing will be held; written comments must include your legal name and civic address.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Submit written comments referencing REZ #24-23 by email to legislativeservices@burnaby.ca, via webform at Burnaby.ca/SubmitALetter, or by mail to Mayor and Council c/o Legislative Services, 4949 Canada Way, Burnaby BC V5G 1M2.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning at 6535 Kingsway in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council will consider first and second reading of Rezoning Bylaw No. 14809 (REZ #25-09) on April 28, 2026 at 5 pm. The proposal would rezone 6535 Kingsway from C7 Drive-In Restaurant to CD Comprehensive Development District to permit a 12-storey mixed-use building with 290 residential units, ground-level commercial space, and underground parking. No public hearing will be held; written comments only are accepted.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Submit written comments by 12:00 noon on Tuesday, April 21, 2026 to be included in the Council package for the April 28 meeting.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning at 2209 and 2225 Springer Avenue, Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on March 24, 2026 at 5 pm on proposed rezoning (REZ #25-20) for 2209 and 2225 Springer Avenue, changing from M2 General Industrial to CD Comprehensive Development District. The plan permits a 49-storey high-rise apartment tower with a 9-storey terraced podium and street-fronting retail. No public hearing will be held; written comments must include your legal name and civic address.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Submit written comments by 12:00 noon on Tuesday, March 24, 2026 to legislativeservices@burnaby.ca or via Burnaby.ca/SubmitALetter.
